content:
# Genesis Chapter 44 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 26r–26v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_044_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_044_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0048 (page 44), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0049 (page 45)

---

## The Cup in the Sack

**1** He gave orders to his steward: "Fill the men's sacks with as much food as they can carry, and put each man's silver back in the mouth of his sack. **2** And put my goblet — the silver goblet — in the mouth of the youngest one's sack, along with his grain silver." And he did as Joseph said.

---

## The Pursuit

**3** At first light, the men were sent off — they and their donkeys. **4** They had barely left the city and had not gone far when Joseph said to his steward, "Get up! Chase after those men! When you catch up with them, say to them: 'Why have you repaid good with evil? **5** Is this not the goblet my master drinks from and uses for divination? You have done a terrible thing.'"

**6** He caught up with them and spoke these words to them. **7** They said to him, "Why does my lord say such things? Far be it from your servants to do such a thing! **8** Look — we even brought back the silver we found in the mouth of our sacks from the land of Canaan. Why would we steal silver or gold from your master's house? **9** If any one of your servants is found with it, let him die — and the rest of us will become my lord's slaves."

**10** "Very well," he said. "Let it be as you say. Whoever is found with it will become my slave, and the rest of you will be free."

**11** They each hurried to lower their sacks to the ground, and each man opened his sack. **12** He searched — beginning with the oldest and ending with the youngest. And the goblet was found in Benjamin's sack.

**13** They tore their garments. Each man loaded his donkey, and they returned to the city.

---

## Before Joseph

**14** Judah and his brothers arrived at Joseph's house — he was still there — and they fell before him on the ground. **15** "What is this you have done?" Joseph said to them. "Did you not know that a man like me can read the hidden things?"

**16** Judah said, "What can we say to my lord? What can we speak? How can we clear ourselves? God has exposed the guilt of your servants. We are now my lord's slaves — we and the one in whose hand the goblet was found."

**17** But he said, "Far be it from me to do such a thing. The man in whose hand the goblet was found — he will be my slave. As for the rest of you, go up in peace to your father."

---

## Judah's Great Speech

**18** Then Judah stepped forward and said: "Please, my lord — let your servant speak a word in my lord's ears, and do not let your anger burn against your servant, for you are like Pharaoh himself.

**19** "My lord asked his servants, 'Do you have a father or a brother?' **20** And we said to my lord, 'We have an aged father, and a young child — a son of his old age. His brother is dead, and he alone is left of his mother's children, and his father loves him.'

**21** "Then you said to your servants, 'Bring him down to me so I may see him with my own eyes.' **22** We said to my lord, 'The boy cannot leave his father. If he were to leave his father, his father would die.' **23** But you said to your servants, 'Unless your youngest brother comes down with you, you will not see my face again.'

**24** "When we went back to your servant my father, we told him the words of my lord. **25** And our father said, 'Go back, buy us a little food.' **26** We said, 'We cannot go down. If our youngest brother is with us, then we will go down — for we cannot see the man's face if our youngest brother is not with us.' **27** And your servant my father said to us, 'You know that my wife bore me two sons. **28** One went away from me and I said, "He must certainly have been torn to pieces," and I have not seen him since. **29** If you take this one too from my presence and something happens to him, you will bring down my gray head in grief to Sheol.'

**30** "So now — when I come to your servant my father and the boy is not with us — since his life is bound up with the boy's life — **31** as soon as he sees that the boy is gone, he will die. And your servants will have brought the gray head of your servant our father down to Sheol in grief. **32** For your servant pledged himself for the boy to my father, saying, 'If I do not bring him back to you, I will bear the blame before my father for all my days.'

**33** "So now — please — let your servant stay instead of the boy as my lord's slave, and let the boy go up with his brothers. **34** For how can I go back to my father if the boy is not with me? I cannot bear to see the ruin that would come upon my father."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The goblet — divination, power, and the hidden trap

**גָּבִיעַ** (*gevi'a*, goblet, cup) — the vessel at the center of chapter 44 — is a word that carries both practical and symbolic weight. The word appears in the Hebrew Bible primarily in two registers: as a cup shape (the "blossom-cups" of the lampstand in Exodus 25:31-34) and as a vessel for liquid. This particular goblet is described as **גְּבִיעִי גְּבִיעַ הַכֶּסֶף** (*gevi'i gevi'a hakesef*, "my goblet, the silver goblet") — the doubling of the word enacts the emphasis: *this specific cup, this particular silver cup*, the one set apart from all others.

In verse 5, Joseph's steward describes the cup as the one the master uses for **נַחֵשׁ יְנַחֵשׁ** (*nachesh yenachesh*, "divination, he practices divination" — the infinitive absolute preceding the finite verb) — the strongest possible assertion in Hebrew grammar. The grammatical construction — infinitive absolute + finite verb — is the same form used in the Ten Commandments' "you shall surely die" and the creation narrative's various emphatic assertions. When the Hebrew Bible wants to say something *absolutely*, it doubles the verb in this way. The steward is not merely claiming that Joseph sometimes uses this cup for divination; he is saying that divination-divination is done with it — that this cup is the divination cup *par excellence*.

This is deeply ironic. Joseph is the man to whom God reveals hidden things through dreams, not through cups. There is no evidence anywhere in the text that Joseph genuinely practiced cup divination (*lecanomancy*, the ancient Near Eastern practice of reading patterns in oil or liquid poured into a basin). The divination claim appears to be part of the theatrical role Joseph has constructed — the omniscient Egyptian official who *knows things* about the brothers before they speak. The divine knowledge Joseph actually possesses is from God. The "divination cup" is the prop that makes that knowledge explicable to Egyptians and terrifying to the brothers.

The cup is planted to do exactly what it does: make an innocent man appear guilty, make the brothers choose between saving their own skins and standing by their youngest brother. This is the test chapter 43 set up with Benjamin's fivefold portion. Now the test goes live.

---

### Vayyiqre'u simlotam — the tearing that echoes chapter 37

**וַיִּקְרְעוּ שִׂמְלֹתָם** — "They tore their garments."

This is one of the most charged four-word phrases in the entire Joseph narrative, and its power comes almost entirely from what the reader knows about the last time garments were torn in this family.

In Genesis 37:29, Reuben returned to the pit and tore his garments when he discovered Joseph gone. In Genesis 37:34, Jacob tore his garments when shown the coat of many colors dipped in blood — the moment he was told his beloved son was dead. The garment-tearing of Genesis 44:13 stands in deliberate echo of both. The same verb (**קָרַע**, *qara'*, to tear), the same word for garments (**שִׂמְלוֹת**, *simlot*), the same corporate grief.

But the difference is devastating in its irony. In chapter 37, the brothers fabricated the evidence — dipped the coat in goat's blood, brought it to their father, watched him tear his garments in grief for a death that had not occurred. Now, in chapter 44, they stand before evidence they did not plant — evidence they cannot explain — and they tear their own garments in the same gesture of grief their father performed for Joseph's pretend death. The brothers are now recipients of the performance they once staged for their father.

The narrator does not editorialize. The verse reads: **וַיִּקְרְעוּ שִׂמְלֹתָם** — *they tore their garments* — and then: **וַיַּעֲמֹס אִישׁ עַל-חֲמֹרוֹ וַיָּשֻׁבוּ הָעִירָה** — *each man loaded his donkey, and they returned to the city*. The grief is performed and then the motion continues. There is nothing to do but go back. The reader is left to feel the weight of the echo the brothers cannot see: they are mourning in the idiom of their father's mourning, which was caused by their own hands, and the brother they lost and who was declared dead is the man they are now returning to face.

---

### "God has exposed the guilt of your servants" — the second confession

**הָאֱלֹהִים מָצָא אֶת-עֲוֺן עֲבָדֶיךָ** — "God has found out the iniquity of your servants."

Judah's response in verse 16 is not a legal defense; it is a theological confession. He does not say: we are innocent, we were framed, someone put the cup in the sack. He says: *God has found out our guilt.* **עָוֺן** (*avon*, iniquity, guilt, the weight of moral failure) is the deepest word in the Hebrew vocabulary for wrongdoing — heavier than *chet'* (missing the mark) and *pesha'* (transgression, rebellion), it carries the sense of a guilt that has accumulated and bent the person under its weight.

This is the brothers' second confession in the Joseph narrative. The first was in Genesis 42:21 — *"We are truly guilty concerning our brother"* — spoken among themselves, out of Joseph's earshot (they thought), when the pressure of their Egyptian imprisonment caused their past to surface. This second confession is public, spoken to the authority who holds their lives. And it goes further: *God has found out our guilt.* The brothers are not merely confessing to circumstantial guilt about the cup; they are confessing to the deep underlying guilt that has been pressing on them for over two decades. They know they are guilty. They do not know of what, exactly, as far as Joseph is concerned. But they know that the **עָוֺן** — the iniquity — is real, and that somehow, inexplicably, the weight of it has caught up with them in Egypt.

Judah uses the verb **מָצָא** (*matsa'*, to find) — God has *found out* their guilt. The same verb appears in chapter 44:9 in the brothers' own ill-fated declaration: *whoever is found with the goblet shall die.* They set the trap of "whoever is found guilty" and now the verb rebounds: God has *found* them. The brothers who declared "let him be found guilty who is found with it" are now confessing that God has *found* them all. The verb is a blade that cuts both ways.

---

### Judah's speech — the transformation of a man

Genesis 44:18-34 is the longest uninterrupted speech in the book of Genesis. It is also the most morally consequential. To read it correctly requires holding in mind simultaneously where Judah *was* in the Joseph narrative and where he is now.

**Where Judah was:** Genesis 37:26-27. The brothers have Joseph in the pit. Reuben is absent. Judah proposes: *"What profit is it if we kill our brother and cover his blood? Come, let us sell him to the Ishmaelites, and let our hand not be against him — for he is our brother, our own flesh."* This speech saved Joseph's life in one sense and destroyed it in another. Judah's motive was not love; it was logic: you do not profit from killing your brother, but you can profit from selling him. The word **בֶּצַע** (*betsa'*, profit, gain, what you get out of it) governs Judah's calculation. He reduced Joseph to a transaction.

Between Genesis 37 and Genesis 44, two things have happened to Judah that the narrator carefully records. In Genesis 38, Judah's own moral failures — his treatment of Tamar, his double standard, his willingness to condemn her to death for a transgression he himself committed — led to his public humiliation and his own confession: **צָדְקָה מִמֶּנִּי** (*tsadeqah mimmeni*, "she is more in the right than I am"). Judah knows what it is to be caught in guilt. He knows what it is to have the hidden thing found out. And in Genesis 43:9, he pledged himself as surety for Benjamin to his father.

Now, in Genesis 44:18-34, the pledge is called. The cup is in Benjamin's sack. Joseph has offered the gracious exit: go home, all of you, the guilty one stays. Judah does not take it. What he does instead is give the speech that is the moral and theological heart of the entire Joseph story.

The speech has three movements:

**Movement 1 — The recall (vv.19-23):** Judah reconstructs the entire conversation from the beginning, from Joseph's initial question about the family through the demand for Benjamin's presence. He is not merely recounting facts; he is establishing *my lord's* responsibility in the chain of events. You asked about the family. You required the youngest to come. We warned you what his absence would cost our father. This is Judah speaking with the confidence of a man who knows the full moral geography of the situation and is not afraid to map it aloud.

**Movement 2 — The father's voice (vv.27-29):** Judah quotes Jacob directly: *"You know that my wife bore me two sons."* This is one of the most emotionally precise moments in the speech. Judah does not paraphrase his father; he *ventriloquizes* him. He makes Joseph hear their father's voice — the old man's description of the two sons of Rachel, the one who went away and was torn to pieces, the gray head that will be driven down to Sheol in grief. Judah is presenting the human cost not abstractly but in the voice of the man who will bear it. The father is not there, but through Judah's speech he becomes present in the room.

**Movement 3 — The substitution (vv.30-34):** Judah lays out the logic of inevitability — if we return without the boy, the father dies; I pledged myself for the boy; therefore I must stay — and then lands on the offer that is the moral climax of the entire narrative: **שְׁבֵנִי-נָא עַבְדְּךָ תַּחַת הַנַּעַר** (*sheveni-na avdekha tachat hana'ar*, "let your servant remain instead of the boy as my lord's slave"). *Instead of.* **תַּחַת** — the Hebrew preposition meaning under, in place of, in substitution for — is the word that closes Judah's moral arc. The man who arranged for his brother to be sold *in exchange for* twenty pieces of silver now offers himself as a slave with no negotiation, no payment, no profit, no **בֶּצַע**. The logic has completely inverted. Where once Judah calculated the gain of selling a brother, now he calculates nothing. He simply offers himself.

The final sentence — **כִּי-אֵיךְ אֶעֱלֶה אֶל-אָבִי וְהַנַּעַר אֵינֶנּוּ אִתִּי פֶּן אֶרְאֶה בָרָע אֲשֶׁר יִמְצָא אֶת-אָבִי** — "For how can I go up to my father if the boy is not with me? I cannot bear to see the ruin that would come upon my father" — is the speech's most extraordinary line. Judah does not say: I pledged myself and must honor my word. He does not say: the utilitarian calculus favors my staying. He says: *I cannot bear to watch what will happen to my father.* The reason he offers himself is not duty or honor or legal obligation. It is that he cannot endure his father's grief.

This is the man who, in chapter 37, sat down to eat after watching his brother disappear into the Ishmaelite caravan, while their father spent years inconsolable. That Judah could eat bread in the face of his father's coming grief. This Judah says: I *cannot bear to see* the ruin. **פֶּן אֶרְאֶה** — "lest I see, I cannot bear to see" — the verb **רָאָה** (*ra'ah*, to see) here carries the sense of witnessing something one cannot endure. Judah is not just describing the practical impossibility of returning without Benjamin. He is confessing that his heart has been so changed that his father's grief has become *intolerable to him*. He would rather be a slave in Egypt than stand in front of his father watching the old man's world collapse.

This is what repentance looks like in the Joseph narrative. Not a statement of remorse. Not a ritual. A man standing before the full consequences of the past and offering himself in place of another.

---

### "Let your servant remain instead of the boy" — substitutionary logic

**שְׁבֵנִי-נָא עַבְדְּךָ תַּחַת הַנַּעַר** — "Let your servant remain instead of the boy."

The word **תַּחַת** (*tachat*) — under, in place of, as a substitute for — is one of the most theologically dense words in the Hebrew Bible. Its basic spatial meaning is "beneath, under." Its legal and relational extension is "in place of, as a substitute, in exchange for." It appears in Genesis 22:13 — the ram offered **תַּחַת בְּנוֹ** (*tachat beno*, "in place of his son") when Isaac was spared on Moriah. It appears in Exodus 21:23-24 — the *lex talionis* formula of eye **תַּחַת** eye, tooth **תַּחַת** tooth, life **תַּחַת** life — the grammar of equivalence and substitution in justice. It appears throughout the sacrificial system in Leviticus and Numbers in the logic of one thing standing in the place of another.

When Judah says *let me stay **tachat** the boy*, he is speaking in the grammar of substitution that runs through the entire Hebrew theological vocabulary. A person standing in the place of another person, absorbing the consequence that would otherwise fall on them. This is the logic of the ram for Isaac, of the sacrificial system, of the servant-songs of Isaiah 53 where the servant bears the iniquity of others **תַּחַת** them. Judah is not drawing on any of these texts — they have not yet been written — but he is enacting the deep structure of the logic they will eventually articulate.

The narrative does not make this substitution theology explicit. It simply records what Judah said. But the reader of the full canon hears in Judah's offer the grammar of a pattern that will be written across the entire Hebrew Bible and will receive its final articulation in the Servant Songs and the New Testament. The moral logic of substitution — one person standing in the place of another, absorbing what the other should bear — is not invented by Judah in Genesis 44. It is *enacted* by him, in the most personal and costly way, in an Egyptian official's audience room, for the sake of a father he loves.

---

### The dramatic irony — Joseph knows what Judah does not

The emotional unbearability of Genesis 44 for the reader depends entirely on dramatic irony. The reader knows what Judah does not know: that the Egyptian official before whom he is standing is Joseph. That the boy the cup was planted on is Benjamin, Joseph's only full brother. That the father whose grief Judah says he cannot endure is the father who has been weeping for Joseph himself for over two decades. That the man who proposes to sell himself as a slave is standing before a man who *was* sold as a slave by his brothers, and who has the power to end the whole test right now.

Joseph has been testing the brothers since chapter 42. The birth-order seating of chapter 43 tested whether their resentment of favoritism remained. The fivefold portion for Benjamin tested it further. Now the cup test takes the testing to its ultimate extreme: here is the one who received the fivefold portion, accused before you, and Joseph is offering you the exit — *go home without him, save yourselves*. The test is: will you abandon the favored son of Rachel when given a clean escape route?

The brothers in chapter 37 did abandon the favored son of Rachel when given no exit at all. Now they are given a clean one. And Judah steps forward — not to negotiate, not to protest their innocence with new evidence, not to appeal to technicalities — but to offer himself in Benjamin's place and to speak the name of the father in the voice of grief.

Joseph is listening to all of this. He has arranged the entire scene to reach this moment. And the scene breaks in the next chapter — he cannot hold himself back. But in chapter 44, he holds. He hears his own story told back to him through Judah's mouth — *one went away from me and I said he must have been torn to pieces and I have not seen him since* — and he holds. The reader watches him hold, and the holding is as devastating as the breaking.

---

### "How can I go up to my father?" — the moral geography of the Joseph story

Judah's final question — **כִּי-אֵיךְ אֶעֱלֶה אֶל-אָבִי** — "For how can I go up to my father?" — is the moral axis of the entire Joseph cycle expressed as a single question.

The verb **עָלָה** (*alah*, to go up) is the standard term for the journey from Egypt to Canaan, since Canaan is at higher elevation. But it carries its full weight here as more than directional travel. *Going up to the father* is the goal toward which this entire seven-chapter narrative has been moving. The father is in Canaan, aged, bereaved, holding together his life around what remains of the sons of Rachel. The brothers have been trying to go back to the father from the beginning — it is why they bought grain, why they returned for the second trip, why everything matters.

Judah's question is not rhetorical in the sense of expecting no answer. It is the revelation of what has changed in him. The old Judah could go up to his father. He had gone up to his father in chapter 37, presumably, with the blood-stained coat and the words "we found this — is this your son's coat?" That Judah could go home. This Judah cannot go home without Benjamin because he cannot bear what the going home would cost the father.

The question stands at the end of the chapter as the last word before Joseph breaks. *How can I go up to my father if the boy is not with me?* The answer is that he cannot. And the man listening to the question is the reason the answer is that he cannot, and is about to become the reason the answer changes.

---

### Structure of the chapter — trial, confession, sacrifice

Genesis 44 has a precise and brutal structure. It is built as a *test of substitution* — the entire chapter is engineered to produce the moment where a brother offers himself in place of the favored son.

**Movement 1 — The setup (vv.1-5):** Joseph plants the cup, sets the trap. The trap is designed with care: the goblet is placed in the last sack searched, the steward's speech includes the gravest possible accusation (divination cup, personal affront to Joseph), and the brothers are overtaken before they have gone far enough to reach safety.

**Movement 2 — The protest and the search (vv.6-12):** The brothers protest their innocence with complete sincerity and extravagant bravado — *let him who has it die, the rest of us will be slaves* — and then watch the cup emerge from Benjamin's sack in the precise reversal of their words. Their generous terms collapse on the one person they cannot afford to lose.

**Movement 3 — The return and the choice (vv.13-17):** The garment-tearing. The return. The audience. Joseph's gracious exit: *go home, only the one found guilty stays*. This is the exact moment the test crystallizes: they can walk out. All of them except Benjamin. The exit is offered.

**Movement 4 — Judah's speech (vv.18-34):** The refusal of the exit. The full speech. The offering of himself. The chapter ends not with resolution but with the speech hanging in the air — Joseph has not yet responded, the brothers do not know what will happen, and the reader is suspended at the precise point of maximum moral clarity before the release of chapter 45.

The chapter is a movement from a planted trap to a freely offered life. Joseph planted the cup in the sack as a device; Judah places himself before Joseph as a person. The artifice of the trap is answered by the authenticity of the sacrifice.

---

### "The one in whose hand the goblet was found" — guilt and innocence inverted

Throughout chapter 44, the language of finding and guilt creates a deliberate disorientation. The brothers declared in verse 9 that whoever *is found* with the cup shall die. In verse 10, the steward modified the terms: whoever is *found* shall be a slave. In verse 12, the cup is *found*. In verse 16, Judah says God has *found* their guilt. The verb **מָצָא** (*matsa'*, to find) appears five times in the chapter, accumulating through each scene until it becomes the word for the intersection of guilt and discovery.

But here is the terrible irony the chapter forces the reader to hold: Benjamin did not take the cup. He is innocent of the charge. The cup was planted. The one who is *found* guilty is the one who is most innocent. And the brothers who are offered freedom are the ones who are genuinely guilty — not of stealing a cup but of the sin for which they confessed in chapter 42 and confessed again in verse 16: the iniquity found out by God.

The chapter works as a moral mirror. The technically guilty (found with the cup) is morally innocent. The technically innocent (not found with the cup) are morally guilty. And into this inversion Judah steps with his offer: let me — who am in the morally guilty category — bear the consequence meant for the one in the technically guilty category, who is morally innocent. Judah is offering to substitute himself for a false conviction to answer for a true one. The complexity of the moral geometry here is breathtaking and the narrator never explains it. He just reports what Judah said.

---

### Manuscript observations

The Leningrad Codex folio 26r (GEN_044_page_001.jpg) carries the end of Genesis 43 in its right column and the opening of Genesis 44 beginning at approximately verse 1. The Genesis 43/44 boundary is marked with the standard Masoretic **פ** (petuhah, open section) indicator visible in the column. The opening of chapter 44 — **וַיְצַו אֶת-אֲשֶׁר עַל-בֵּיתוֹ** (*vayetsav et asher al-beyto*, "he commanded the one over his house") — is legible in the Tiberian vocalization in the right column.

The divination cup description in verse 2 — **גְּבִיעִי גְּבִיעַ הַכֶּסֶף** (*gevi'i gevi'a hakesef*, "my goblet, the silver goblet") — with its doubled noun showing the emphatic identification of the specific vessel, is visible in the second column of the folio with the characteristic *gimel-bet-yod-ayin* consonants of *gevi'a*.

The infinitive absolute construction in verse 5 — **נַחֵשׁ יְנַחֵשׁ** (*nachesh yenachesh*, "he practices divination, indeed divines") — is legible in the Leningrad Codex pointing with the distinctive Tiberian vowels on both the infinitive absolute and the finite verb forms of the root **נ-ח-שׁ** (*nun-het-shin*, the divination root).

The brothers' innocent protest in verses 7-9 occupies the central portion of the first page's right column, with the formal legal language of the self-imposed penalty clearly pointed: **יָמוּת** (*yamut*, let him die) in verse 9 and **נִהְיֶה לַאדֹנִי לַעֲבָדִים** (*nihyeh la'adoni la'avadim*, "we will be slaves to my lord") both legible.

The pivotal garment-tearing verse — **וַיִּקְרְעוּ שִׂמְלֹתָם** (*vayyiqre'u simlotam*, v.13) — is visible in the left column of folio 26r. The Qal imperfect of **קָרַע** (*qara'*, to tear) with the *vav*-consecutive and the plural form of **שִׂמְלוֹת** (*simlot*, garments) are legible, the same verb and noun that appeared in Genesis 37:34 for Jacob's mourning.

Folio 26v (GEN_044_page_002.jpg) carries Judah's great speech from verse 18 through verse 34 and into the beginning of Genesis 45. The speech occupies the majority of the right column and flows into the middle column, its length unusual in the narrative portions of Genesis — most individual speeches run a few verses; Judah's occupies seventeen verses across the folio. The verse boundaries within the speech are marked by the characteristic Masoretic cantillation marks (te'amim) and occasionally by the small Masoretic division markers.

The climactic substitution phrase in verse 33 — **שְׁבֵנִי-נָא עַבְדְּךָ תַּחַת הַנַּעַר** (*sheveni-na avdekha tachat hana'ar*, "please let your servant remain instead of the boy") — is legible in the right column of folio 26v with the particle of entreaty **נָא** (*na*, please), the distinctive **תַּחַת** (*tachat*, in place of), and the noun **נַּעַר** (*na'ar*, youth/boy) all visible in the careful Tiberian pointing.

The final verse — Judah's closing question **כִּי-אֵיךְ אֶעֱלֶה אֶל-אָבִי** (*ki eikh e'eleh el-avi*, "for how can I go up to my father?") — falls near the end of the right column of folio 26v, with the interrogative **אֵיךְ** (*eikh*, how?) giving the verse its characteristic cadence. The chapter then flows directly into Genesis 45 in the following lines, consistent with the narrative's refusal to pause between Judah's question and Joseph's answer.

Notable LXX (Vaticanus) variants for Genesis 44:

**V. 5** — For the infinitive absolute construction **נַחֵשׁ יְנַחֵשׁ** (*nachesh yenachesh*, "he practices divination, divines indeed"), the Vaticanus text on pa_0044 reads **οἰωνισμῷ οἰωνίζεται** (*oiōnismō oionizetai*, "by divination he divines") — a near-exact replication of the Hebrew grammatical strategy using the Greek dative cognate figure (*figura etymologica*): the cognate noun in the dative case preceding the finite verb. The LXX translator understood the infinitive absolute construction and found a Greek equivalent that preserves the emphasis through the same mechanism: noun and verb from the same root in immediate succession. The word **οἰωνίζομαι** (*oiōnizomai*) means to take omens from the flight of birds (*oionos*, bird of omen), a slightly different form of divination than the cup-reading implied in the Hebrew — but the LXX translator chose the Greek divination word most natural to a Greek reader.

**V. 16** — For Judah's confession **הָאֱלֹהִים מָצָא אֶת-עֲוֺן עֲבָדֶיךָ** (*ha-Elohim matsa et avon avadekha*, "God has found out the iniquity of your servants"), Vaticanus reads **ὁ θεὸς εὗρεν τὴν ἀδικίαν τῶν παίδων σου** — "God has found the injustice/unrighteousness of your servants." The LXX renders **עָוֺן** (*avon*, iniquity) as **ἀδικία** (*adikia*, injustice, unrighteousness) rather than the more common **ἁμαρτία** (*hamartia*, sin) or **ἀνομία** (*anomia*, lawlessness). The choice of **ἀδικία** — a legal and relational word meaning failure of justice, failure to give what is due — captures one dimension of **avon** accurately: the brothers have failed in their duty to Joseph and to their father. However, **avon** in Hebrew also carries the sense of the psychological weight of guilt that bends the person under it, a nuance that **ἀδικία** does not fully convey. The LXX translation is correct in its legal register and slightly reduced in its psychological one.

**V. 18** — Judah's opening approach is rendered in Vaticanus as **προσελθὼν δὲ αὐτῷ Ιουδας εἶπεν** (*proselthōn de autō Ioudas eipen*, "and Judah, approaching him, said"). The verb **προσελθών** (*proselthōn*, having approached, drawing near) renders the Hebrew **וַיִּגַּשׁ אֵלָיו יְהוּדָה** (*vayyiggash elav Yehudah*, "and Judah drew near to him"). The Hebrew **נָגַשׁ** (*nagash*, to draw near, approach) in narrative contexts often carries the weight of a deliberate, consequential approach — the same verb used for Abraham drawing near (*vayyiggash*, Genesis 18:23) to intercede for Sodom before the LORD. Judah's approach to Joseph is an act of intercession, and the verb signals it. The Vaticanus **προσελθών** captures the directional movement but somewhat flattens the weight of the deliberate act.

**V. 30** — For the phrase describing Jacob's soul bound with Benjamin's soul — **וְנַפְשׁוֹ קְשׁוּרָה בְנַפְשׁוֹ** (*venafshov qeshurah venafshov*, "and his life/soul is bound up with his life/soul") — Vaticanus reads **ἡ δὲ ψυχὴ αὐτοῦ ἐκκέκρεμαι τῆς τούτου ψυχῆς** — "and his soul/life hangs suspended from this one's soul." The Greek verb **ἐκκρέμαμαι** (*ekkremamai*, to hang from, be suspended from) is a remarkable translation choice. Where the Hebrew **קָשַׁר** (*qashar*, to bind, tie, knot) describes the bond as a knot or tie — two things fastened together — the LXX renders it as one soul *hanging from* another: the father's life as dependent, suspended, held up by the son's existence. The image is spatial and vertiginous: if the supporting soul is removed, the suspended soul falls. This is a different metaphor than the Hebrew but not inaccurate to the emotional reality. Both the binding and the suspension describe a life made contingent on another life.

**V. 33** — For the substitution formula **שְׁבֵנִי-נָא עַבְדְּךָ תַּחַת הַנַּעַר** (*sheveni-na avdekha tachat hana'ar*, "please let your servant remain instead of the boy"), Vaticanus reads **μεινάτω ὁ παῖς σου ἀντὶ τοῦ παιδὸς οἰκέτης τῷ κυρίῳ** — "let your servant remain as a household slave to the lord instead of the boy." The Vaticanus chooses **ἀντί** (*anti*, instead of, in place of, in exchange for) to render **תַּחַת** (*tachat*). Both **תַּחַת** and **ἀντί** are the standard substitution prepositions in their respective languages — the *lex talionis* of Exodus uses **תַּחַת** while the NT uses **ἀντί** in substitutionary atonement language (Mark 10:45: **δοῦναι τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ λύτρον ἀντὶ πολλῶν**, "to give his life as a ransom *anti* many"). Vaticanus's rendering of Judah's offer with **ἀντί** places it in the precise grammatical register that the New Testament will use for the substitutionary self-offering of the Servant. The LXX translator may not have been making a theological point; he was choosing the natural Greek substitution preposition. But the result is that the Greek Vaticanus has Judah offering himself with the exact word Paul and Mark will use for Jesus's self-offering.

The Vaticanus pages covering Genesis 44 (pa_0044–pa_0045, accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0048 and https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0049) present the text in continuous uncial script without word division in the characteristic three-column layout. The chapter marker visible in the upper portion of pa_0044 corresponds to the Vaticanus internal chapter numbering. Judah's speech in the Greek runs visibly longer on pa_0045 than surrounding narrative passages, the seventeen-verse speech occupying a substantial portion of the left and center columns of that page before Genesis 45 begins in the right column.
